title: The Effects of Menstrual Cycle Phase on Exercise Performance in Eumenorrheic Women: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
note: Central anchor: pooled effect of MC phase on performance is trivial; large between-study variance. Grounds the 'weak/inconsistent phase effect' thesis.

title: The Effects of Oral Contraceptives on Exercise Performance in Women: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
note: Companion MA: OC use may trivially reduce performance vs. natural cycle; most studies low quality/underpowered.

title: Current evidence shows no influence of women's menstrual cycle phase on acute strength performance or adaptations to resistance exercise training
container-title: Frontiers in Sports and Active Living
note: Argues phase does not meaningfully affect acute strength or RT adaptation; critiques methodology of pro-phase studies.

title: Evaluation the Impact of Hormonal Fluctuations During the Menstrual Cycle on the Performance of Female Athletes - Systematic Review
note: Recent SR (n=13 studies, 152 athletes); reports inconsistent phase effects. NOTE: MDPI publisher - flag for publisher-vetting per inclusion rules.

title: The Effects of Menstrual Cycle Phase on Elite Athlete Performance: A Critical and Systematic Review
note: Critical SR focused on elite athletes; author order to confirm on import.

title: Effects on power, strength and lean body mass of menstrual/oral contraceptive cycle based resistance training
container-title: Journal of Sports Medicine and Physical Fitness
note: MANDATORY pro-phase counter-anchor: reports follicular-phase-based training superior. Engaged directly; methodological limits (BBT-only phase ID) noted in rebuttals.

title: Effects of follicular versus luteal phase-based strength training in young women
note: MANDATORY pro-phase counter-anchor: follicular-phase training gave greater strength/hypertrophy in a within-subject leg design.

title: Effects of Follicular and Luteal Phase-Based Menstrual Cycle Resistance Training on Muscle Strength and Mass
note: MANDATORY counter-anchor: found no benefit of phase-based over regular training; contradicts Sung/Wikstrom-Frisen with tighter design.

title: Effects of Menstrual Phase-Dependent Resistance Training Frequency on Muscular Hypertrophy and Strength
container-title: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
note: Null result: no effect of phase-dependent training frequency on hypertrophy. Balances the pro-phase anchors.

title: Does the Menstrual Cycle Influence Aerobic Capacity in Endurance-Trained Women?
container-title: Research Quarterly for Exercise and Sport
note: Endurance-specific: aerobic capacity across phases in trained women.

title: Understanding the female athlete: molecular mechanisms underpinning menstrual phase differences in exercise metabolism
note: Mechanistic review of substrate metabolism across the cycle; underpins any nutrition-timing rationale.

title: Menstrual and oral contraceptive cycle phases do not affect submaximal and maximal exercise responses
container-title: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports
note: Well-controlled null: no phase effect on sub-/maximal responses.

title: International society of sports nutrition position stand: nutritional concerns of the female athlete
container-title: Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition
note: ISSN position stand. Author list truncated (et al.) - confirm on import. Notably endorses individualized tracking over rigid phase prescription.

title: Fueling the Female Athlete: Auditing Her Representation in Studies of Acute Carbohydrate Intake for Exercise
note: Audit of female representation in carbohydrate-intake research; nutrition + evidence-gap.

title: Auditing the Representation of Female Versus Male Athletes in Sports Science and Sports Medicine Research: Evidence-Based Performance Supplements
note: Supplement-research audit. MDPI - flag for publisher vetting.

title: The IOC consensus statement: beyond the Female Athlete Triad - Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S)
note: Seminal RED-S consensus (pre-15yr, retained as foundational). Author list truncated.

title: IOC consensus statement on relative energy deficiency in sport (RED-S): 2018 update
note: 2018 RED-S update. Author list truncated.

title: 2023 International Olympic Committee's (IOC) consensus statement on Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (REDs)
note: Current REDs consensus; updated conceptual + physiological models. Author list truncated.

title: Energy availability in athletes
note: Seminal energy-availability review (pre-15yr, retained as foundational).

title: Low Energy Availability Is Difficult to Assess but Outcomes Have Large Impact on Bone Injury Rates in Elite Distance Athletes
container-title: International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism
note: Links low energy availability to bone-injury outcomes in elite athletes.

title: Identifying latent classes of Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (REDs) consequences in a sample of collegiate female cross country runners
note: Empirical REDs consequence clustering in female runners.

title: High prevalence of subtle and severe menstrual disturbances in exercising women: confirmation using daily hormone measures
note: Seminal: menstrual disturbances common in exercising women (pre-15yr, retained). Author list truncated.

title: The Prevalence and Impact of Heavy Menstrual Bleeding (Menorrhagia) in Elite and Non-Elite Athletes
note: Heavy menstrual bleeding prevalence/impact in athletes; symptom burden evidence.

title: Inconsistencies in the perceived impact of the menstrual cycle on sport performance and in the prevalence of menstrual cycle symptoms: A scoping review of the literature
container-title: Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport
note: Key perceived-impact review: 2.8-100% report negative impact; symptoms, not phase per se, drive perception.

title: Perceived impact of the menstrual cycle and hormonal contraceptives on physical exercise and performance in 1,086 athletes from 57 sports
note: Large survey (n=1,086): symptoms relate to perceived performance; only 18% factor cycle into planning.

title: Prevalence and frequency of menstrual cycle symptoms are associated with availability to train and compete: a study of 6,812 exercising women recruited using the Strava exercise app
note: Large app-recruited cohort (n=6,812): symptom burden linked to reduced availability to train/compete. Author list truncated.

title: Menstrual Cycle: The Importance of Both the Phases and the Transitions Between Phases on Training and Performance
note: Argues transitions between phases (not just phases) matter for individualized planning.

title: Menstrual cycle and hormonal contraceptive phases' effect on elite rowers' training, performance and wellness
note: Longitudinal elite-rower monitoring of cycle/HC effects on wellness + performance. Author list truncated.

title: Period Prevalence and Perceived Side Effects of Hormonal Contraceptive Use and the Menstrual Cycle in Elite Athletes
container-title: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
note: HC prevalence + perceived side effects in elite athletes.

title: How regular is regular? An analysis of menstrual cycle regularity
note: Seminal: quantifies cycle irregularity, undermining the fixed-28-day assumption (pre-15yr, retained).

title: Real-world menstrual cycle characteristics of more than 600,000 menstrual cycles
note: App-derived large-scale data on real cycle variability; supports individualized tracking over population assumptions.

title: Where are all the female participants in Sports and Exercise Medicine research?
note: Seminal quantification of female underrepresentation (the original 'sex data gap' paper).

title: 'Invisible Sportswomen': The Sex Data Gap in Sport and Exercise Science Research
container-title: Women in Sport and Physical Activity Journal
note: Flagship follow-up to Costello: only 6% of studies female-only; 34% of participants female across 12.5M.

title: Methodological Recommendations for Menstrual Cycle Research in Sports and Exercise
note: Gold-standard methods guide for MC research; defines phase-verification standards.

title: Methodological Considerations for Studies in Sport and Exercise Science with Women as Participants: A Working Guide for Standards of Practice for Research on Women
note: Standards-of-practice guide for female-participant research; core methods citation.

title: Elite female athlete research: stop searching for the 'magic P'
note: Commentary against over-reliance on phase p-values; argues for individualized approaches.

title: Dealing with menstrual cycle in sport: stop finding excuses to exclude women from research
note: Commentary countering the use of cycle complexity as a reason to exclude women.

title: Sex Bias in Cohorts Included in Sports Medicine Research
note: Quantifies male-skewed cohorts in sports-medicine research.

title: Sport Science on Women, Women in Sport Science
container-title: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
note: Editorial framing the female evidence gap and the researcher-gender dimension.

title: Resistance training alters body composition in middle-aged women depending on menopause - A 20-week control trial
note: RT effects on body composition differ pre- vs post-menopause; hypertrophy seen only pre-menopause.

title: A Novel Low-Impact Resistance Exercise Program Increases Strength and Balance in Females Irrespective of Menopause Status
note: RT improves strength/balance regardless of menopause status; practical for the perimenopause track.

title: American College of Sports Medicine position stand. The female athlete triad
note: Seminal ACSM position stand defining the Female Athlete Triad continuum (pre-15yr, retained as foundational).

title: 2014 Female Athlete Triad Coalition Consensus Statement on Treatment and Return to Play of the Female Athlete Triad
note: Triad Coalition clinical consensus on treatment/return-to-play (pre-15yr, retained). Author list truncated.

title: Prevalence of individual and combined components of the female athlete triad
note: Prevalence data for triad components across athlete samples.

title: The Effect of Menstrual Cycle and Contraceptives on ACL Injuries and Laxity: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
note: MA: knee laxity increases at ovulation vs. follicular; ACL-injury association at very low strength of evidence.

title: Effect of Estrogen on Musculoskeletal Performance and Injury Risk
note: Mechanistic review: estrogen raises muscle strength but lowers tendon/ligament stiffness (injury-risk tradeoff).

title: Exercise-Induced Muscle Damage During the Menstrual Cycle: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
container-title: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
note: MA: DOMS and strength loss highest in early follicular, lowest mid-luteal; NOT reflected in creatine kinase (perceived vs. objective dissociation) - a core symptom-responsive anchor.

title: Menstrual Cycle Symptoms and Sleep: Associations With Performance and Recovery in Elite Athletes
container-title: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
note: Symptom-sleep-recovery associations in elite athletes; supports symptom (not phase) as the actionable signal.

title: The Impact of Menstrual Cycle Phase on Athletes' Performance: A Narrative Review
note: Widely-cited narrative review of phase effects; concludes individualization over rigid prescription. MDPI - flag.

title: The Menstrual Cycle, Exercise Performance and Recovery: Narrative Review and Practical Recommendations
note: Practitioner-facing narrative review with applied recommendations. MDPI - flag.

title: The Effect of Hormonal Contraceptive Use on Skeletal Muscle Hypertrophy, Power and Strength Adaptations to Resistance Exercise Training: A Systematic Review and Multilevel Meta-analysis
note: MA: HC use does not meaningfully alter hypertrophy/power/strength adaptation to resistance training.

title: Effects of oral contraceptive use on muscle strength, muscle thickness, and fiber size and composition in young women undergoing 12 weeks of strength training: a cohort study
note: Cohort: OC vs non-OC fiber-type/strength adaptations over 12 wk of RT.

title: Behaviour change techniques in cardiovascular disease smartphone apps to improve physical activity and sedentary behaviour: Systematic review and meta-regression
container-title: International Journal of Behavioral Nutrition and Physical Activity
note: Which BCTs drive PA in apps: action planning and self-monitoring most effective - directly informs Axis feature design.

title: Quality, Features, and Presence of Behavior Change Techniques in Mobile Apps Designed to Improve Physical Activity in Pregnant Women: Systematic Search and Content Analysis
note: Content analysis showing commercial PA apps often lack evidence-based BCTs. Author list truncated.

title: Machine learning-based menstrual phase identification using wearable device data
note: Wrist-wearable signals (skin temp, EDA, HR) classify cycle phase at 87% (3-phase). Authors resolve via DOI on import - directly relevant to Axis's passive-input goal.

title: Menstrual Cycle Symptom Tracking and the Association With Training Load and Wellness in Elite Female Athletes
container-title: Frontiers in Sports and Active Living
note: Symptom tracking relates to training load and wellness in elite athletes - evidence base for symptom-driven load adjustment.

title: Menstrual cycle monitoring in applied sport settings: A scoping review
container-title: International Journal of Sports Science & Coaching
note: Scoping review of how MC monitoring is applied in sport; argues for evidence-based-but-flexible, individualized tracking.

title: Flowing data: women's views and experiences on privacy and data security when using menstrual cycle tracking apps
note: Qualitative evidence on user privacy/data-security concerns - grounds Axis's trust-as-a-feature positioning. Authors resolve via DOI.

title: Influence of menstrual phase and arid vs. humid heat stress on autonomic and behavioural thermoregulation during exercise in trained but unacclimated women
note: Thermoregulatory responses across MC phases during heat-stress exercise in trained women.

title: Temperature regulation in women: Effects of the menstrual cycle
note: Comprehensive review of core-temperature rhythms and thermoregulation across the cycle. Authors resolve via DOI on import.

title: Three-step method for menstrual and oral contraceptive cycle verification
container-title: Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport
note: The gold-standard three-step phase-verification method (mapping + urinary LH + serum hormones). Core methods reference for any phase-based claim.

title: Level of Agreement Between a Modified, Three-Step Menstrual Cycle Tracking Method and a Female-Health Menstrual Cycle Tracking App
note: Benchmarks a cycle-tracking app's phase identification against modified three-step verification - directly relevant to Axis's tracking accuracy.

title: Is iron treatment beneficial in, iron-deficient but non-anaemic (IDNA) endurance athletes? A systematic review and meta-analysis
note: BJSM MA on iron treatment in non-anaemic iron-deficient endurance athletes; menstrual blood loss is a key female-athlete risk factor.

title: ROAR: How to Match Your Food and Fitness to Your Unique Female Physiology for Optimum Performance, Great Health, and a Strong, Lean Body for Life
note: EXPERT-OPINION (Tier 3): PhD exercise physiologist, mainstream publisher (Rodale/Penguin Random House). Practical/translational - NOT to support efficacy claims alone; cross-check against primary literature. Revised ed. 2024 (ISBN 9780593581926).

title: Next Level: Your Guide to Kicking Ass, Feeling Great, and Crushing Goals Through Menopause and Beyond
note: EXPERT-OPINION (Tier 3): qualified author, mainstream publisher. Perimenopause/menopause training + nutrition. Practical guidance, not primary evidence.
